macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi's statements on Birkas Kohanim: Generosity and communal responsibility

micro_segments.title: Introduction to Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi's teachings on the character required for giving blessings
The connection between stinginess (tzar ayin) and the need for egla arufa in cases of unsolved murder
The scenario of abandonment: the traveler who receives no food or accompaniment and its consequences

macro_segments.title: Birkas Kohanim in a congregation of all Kohanim: Who receives the blessing?

micro_segments.title: The case of a synagogue where all members are kohanim and the question of who receives the blessing
Rav Zeira's answer: the blessing goes to brothers in the fields and to the absent who are part of the community
The resolution based on whether absence is coerced or voluntary (ones vs. reshus)
Rabbi Simi's teaching: splitting the kohanim so some remain below as a receiving congregation
The requirement of exactly ten or more than ten in order to maintain a minyan below
Discussion of the principle that Birkas Kohanim requires a kahal (congregation) to receive it

macro_segments.title: Who is excluded from Birkas Kohanim: Position relative to the kohanim

micro_segments.title: Those standing behind the kohanim are excluded from the blessing; physical positioning matters
Tall people in front do not interrupt (hefsek); the teivah does not interrupt
Tosafos's principle: being behind kohanim shows you've excluded yourself from community
An iron wall (mechitzah) does not separate Israel from their Father in Heaven; the barrier is not absolute
Tosafos's distinction: mechitzah between coerced (ones) and voluntary absence; the core minyan must be together
Beautiful passage from Tosafos: God's perspective transcends physical barriers; what matters is the relationship to God
Question raised: those standing to the side (tzadadim) of the kohanim—are they included?
Case of sprinkling parah adumah: side positioning is included in front positioning (kesherah)

macro_segments.title: The requirement for kohanim to wash their hands before Birkas Kohanim

micro_segments.title: A kohen who has not washed his hands cannot lift his hands in blessing; source from "Lift up your hands, holy"
Rashi's interpretation: washing must occur immediately before ascending to the duchan
Tosafos's analysis: clarifying whether morning washing suffices and questioning Rashi's gloss
The bracha before Birkas Kohanim: Asher kidshanu b'mitzvosav u'bikdushaso shel Aharon v'tzivanu l'varech
Significance of 'u'bikdushaso shel Aharon' in the bracha: specifying the particular sanctity of kohanim
The phrase 'b'ahava' (with love) in the blessing reflects the earlier teaching about tov ayin and ahava

macro_segments.title: Choreography and procedures for Birkas Kohanim: Step-by-step observances

micro_segments.title: As the kohanim begin to move their legs toward the duchan, they recite Yehi ratzon requesting no stumbling or sin in the blessing
Prayer said when facing away: 'Ribbono Shel Olam asinu mah she'gazarta aleinu' acknowledging the distinctive role
When kohanim turn their faces away from the congregation after the blessing, they do not bend their fingers until turning away
Rav Chisda's strict rules on sequencing: the reader must finish before kohanim begin; kahal must finish their Amen before the next bracha
Kohanim cannot leave the duchan until the shliach tzibur begins Sim Shalom
Tosafos's explanation: the concern is not about two voices being heard, but about each person being able to hear when they should

macro_segments.title: Related halakhic principles: Analogous choreography and respect during Torah reading and haftarah

micro_segments.title: One must read from Torah before giving the haftarah; each ritual act has its proper sequence
The maftir must complete rolling the Torah scroll before beginning the haftarah blessing
The shliach tzibur may not remove the decorative coverings from the teivah while the congregation is present (kvod hatzibur)
The congregation may not leave until the Torah scroll is removed and restored; disagreement between Tanna and Shmuel on timing
Principle: Acharei Hashem Elokeichem teleichu—follow after God; the Torah goes first, then the people

topical_tags.term: Egla arufa (heifer ritual for unsolved murder)
Yehi ratzon (may it be Your will)
Ribbono Shel Olam (Master of the Universe)
Sequencing and choreography (strict order of ritual)
Trei kali lo mishtamei (two voices not heard)
Acharei Hashem Elokeichem teleichu (follow after God)
